metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how the `ensures:` summary lines are handled in `--output json` mode: either explicitly document that they are intentionally absent from the JSON envelope (with a note in the plan), or add them as a dedicated top-level key (e.g. `"ensures": {"applied": N, "total": M}`) in `_transform_json`'s output schema.
findings.finding: The `ensures:` summary lines (both the read-only `ensures: N/M applied` line from `_scan` and the `--repair` sweep summary line) start with a lowercase word, so `_transform_json`'s regex `^([A-Z_]+):\s*` will not match them and they will be silently dropped from `--output json` output. The plan never addresses this: it says the line is 'informational' but does not specify whether it should appear in the JSON envelope, be added to a new key, or be intentionally omitted. JSON consumers (e.g. CI scripts calling `rebar fsck --output json`) will get no ensures signal at all.
findings.location: What section — 'Read-only ensures: N/M applied line' and 'Fold run_ensures() into fsck --repair' subsections; `_transform_json` at fsck.py:486–513

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a test (or explicit rationale for its absence) in the plan that exercises the MCP server startup code path — at minimum a unit test that monkeypatches `run_ensures` to raise and asserts `build_server().run()` is still reached, satisfying the 'never aborts boot' guarantee.
findings.finding: AC4 (MCP server startup best-effort sweep) covers a new code path cutover but names no test in `test_fsck_ensures.py` and no end-to-end proving command; the only new test file is fsck-scoped, leaving the MCP boot path unexercised by any described check.
findings.location: AC4; Tests section ('WS3 adds a NEW `tests/interfaces/store/test_fsck_ensures.py`')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a test case to `tests/interfaces/store/test_fsck_ensures.py` (or a new `test_mcp_startup_ensures.py`) that patches `ensures.run_ensures` to raise an arbitrary exception and asserts `mcp_server.main` does not re-raise; list this in AC4 as the proving check.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add test coverage for the MCP server startup ensure-sweep path: at minimum (a) happy-path: sweep runs and completes before build_server().run(); (b) failure-path: ImportError / missing store / sweep error logs and does NOT abort boot; (c) short-lock / timeout: a contended lock skips rather than blocking indefinitely. These can be offline/unit tests using a mock run_ensures, but the behavioral contracts (log-and-continue, lock not held across run()) must be asserted, not just the happy path.
findings.finding: MCP server startup introduces a new defaulted code path (called on every server boot) with several specified behavioral contracts (log-and-continue on ImportError/missing-store/sweep-error, short lock budget skips rather than delays, lock released before build_server().run()), but no test file or test cases are described anywhere in the plan for this surface — AC5 and the named test file (test_fsck_ensures.py) explicitly cover only fsck surfaces, leaving MCP startup behavior entirely untested.
findings.location: AC4 (MCP server startup) and AC5 / Tests section
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `tests/interfaces/server/test_mcp_startup_ensures.py` (or extend the existing MCP server test if one exists) with three parametrized cases: (1) run_ensures succeeds — assert build_server().run() is still called; (2) run_ensures raises an exception — assert boot continues (no re-raise); (3) run_ensures is called with timeout/attempts=1 — assert the kwarg is forwarded. Update AC5 to reference this file explicitly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `rebar._store.ensures` (with `run_ensures`, `registry_ids`, `applied_ids`, `EnsureOutcome`) actually exists on the branch WS3 will build on, or explicitly gate WS3 as requiring WS1 to be merged first and add a CI dependency check.
findings.finding: The plan's opening 'What' paragraph makes a confident codebase assertion — 'WS1 landed `rebar._store.ensures` with `run_ensures(tracker) -> list[EnsureOutcome]`, `registry_ids() -> frozenset[str]`, and `applied_ids(tracker) -> set[str]`' — but probing `src/rebar/_store/` finds no `ensures.py`, and a repo-wide search for `run_ensures`, `registry_ids`, `applied_ids`, and `EnsureOutcome` all return zero hits in source files. WS3's entire implementation (all three surfaces: fsck --repair phase, N/M line, MCP startup) imports from this module; if WS1 has not yet delivered it, WS3 has no callable to wire.
findings.location: What section, paragraph 1: 'WS1 landed `rebar._store.ensures` with `run_ensures(tracker) -> list[EnsureOutcome]`, `registry_ids() -> frozenset[str]`, and `applied_ids(tracker) -> set[str]`'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an explicit prerequisite statement: 'WS3 requires WS1 to be merged; confirm `src/rebar/_store/ensures.py` is present and exports `run_ensures`, `registry_ids`, `applied_ids`, `EnsureOutcome` before starting WS3 work.' Alternatively, if WS1 and WS3 are developed in parallel on a feature branch, add an import guard in the fsck and MCP wiring that fails loudly (not silently) if the module is absent.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm `rebar._store.ensures` (with `run_ensures`, `registry_ids`, `applied_ids`, `EnsureOutcome`) is landed and importable before WS3 work begins, and add an explicit merge-order gate in the Scope section.
findings.finding: `rebar._store.ensures` and all its named exports (`run_ensures`, `registry_ids()`, `applied_ids()`, `EnsureOutcome`) do not exist anywhere in the codebase (`src/rebar/_store/` has no `ensures.py`). The plan's entire WS3 edit-set imports and calls these symbols; if WS1 has not yet landed, every edit target in WS3 is unreachable.
findings.location: What section — 'WS1 landed `rebar._store.ensures` with `run_ensures(tracker) -> list[EnsureOutcome]`, `registry_ids() -> frozenset[str]`, and `applied_ids(tracker) -> set[str]`'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope: 'Prerequisite: WS1 must be merged and `rebar._store.ensures` importable before any WS3 edit is started. Verify with `python -c "from rebar._store import ensures; ensures.registry_ids()"`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document the exact WS1 interface contract (run_ensures signature including timeout/attempts kwargs, EnsureOutcome fields, and the guarantee that the write lock is acquired AND released internally per call) as a hard prerequisite spec before WS3 begins implementation, so the fsck and MCP surfaces can be verified against an audited contract rather than an assumed one.
findings.finding: The entire foundational API of rebar._store.ensures — run_ensures(), registry_ids(), applied_ids(), and EnsureOutcome — does not exist in the codebase (src/rebar/_store/ has no ensures.py). The plan treats these as 'WS1 landed' capabilities, but since WS1 is a separate workstream and not yet present, every plan assertion about the interface (lock ownership, return shape, timeout/attempts signature, per-sweep atomicity) is an unverified capability claim WS3 directly builds on.
findings.location: What section (all three surfaces); AC lines 1, 2, 3, 4, 5
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'WS1 interface contract' subsection to the plan (or reference a WS1 AC) that pins: the exact `run_ensures(tracker, *, timeout=None, attempts=None) -> list[EnsureOutcome]` signature, the EnsureOutcome.status values (ok/changed/failed), and the explicit lock invariant (lock acquired and released inside run_ensures, never leaked to caller). Gate WS3 on WS1's AC being green.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `rebar._store.ensures` (with `run_ensures`, `registry_ids`, `applied_ids`, `EnsureOutcome`) is merged and importable before WS3 implementation begins, or add an explicit WS1→WS3 dependency gate in the plan with a fallback import guard.
findings.finding: The entire WS3 implementation depends on `rebar._store.ensures` (specifically `run_ensures`, `registry_ids`, `applied_ids`, and `EnsureOutcome`) which does not exist in the current codebase — `src/rebar/_store/` has no `ensures.py` and searches for all four symbols return zero source-code matches. The plan asserts 'WS1 landed rebar._store.ensures' as an established fact, but this is an unverified external prerequisite from a parallel/prior workstream. If WS1 has not merged before WS3 implementation begins, every call site (fsck --repair sweep, _scan N/M line, mcp_server.main startup) will fail with ImportError at runtime.
findings.location: What section — 'WS1 landed `rebar._store.ensures` with `run_ensures(tracker) -> list[EnsureOutcome]`, `registry_ids() -> frozenset[str]`, and `applied_ids(tracker) -> set[str]`'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a WS ordering statement: 'WS3 is blocked on WS1 merge; implementors must confirm `from rebar._store.ensures import run_ensures, registry_ids, applied_ids` succeeds before touching fsck.py or mcp_server.py.' Alternatively, add a try/except ImportError guard in fsck_cli's repair block (mirroring the MCP startup guard) so a missing WS1 degrades gracefully rather than crashing the CLI.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define `EnsureOutcome`'s field names and exhaustive status vocabulary (`ok`, `changed`, `failed`, and any others) in WS3's plan (or by explicit cross-reference to a WS1 spec section), so the `C`/`F`/`N` counting and per-unit reporting rules are unambiguously implementable.
findings.finding: `EnsureOutcome`'s value vocabulary (the field names and the exhaustive set of status values — implied to be `ok`, `changed`, `failed`) is never defined in this plan (or verifiably anywhere in the codebase, since `rebar._store.ensures` does not exist). The plan's report format `ensures: swept N unit(s); C changed, F failed` and the per-unit-non-ok reporting rule both require an executor to know which field on an `EnsureOutcome` carries the status and what the complete set of status values is.
findings.location: What section — 'Fold `run_ensures()` into `fsck --repair`': "call `ensures.run_ensures(tracker)` and append a short summary of the outcomes to the report (`ensures: swept N unit(s); C changed, F failed`, plus a per-unit line for any non-`ok` unit)"
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-paragraph addendum to the 'What / Fold run_ensures' section specifying `EnsureOutcome` as e.g. `@dataclass class EnsureOutcome: unit_id: str; status: Literal['ok', 'changed', 'failed']; detail: str = ''` and confirm that `C = sum(o.status == 'changed' ...)`, `F = sum(o.status == 'failed' ...)`, `N = len(outcomes)`, non-ok means `status != 'ok'`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the fsck exit code when ensure units fail (F > 0) during `--repair` — document whether the existing fsck non-zero exit convention applies, and add an AC asserting the exit code behavior for the failure case.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify the fsck exit code when ensure units fail during `--repair` (i.e., F > 0 in `ensures: swept N; C changed, F failed`). If the process exits 0 despite failed ensures, CI pipelines and operators receive no actionable signal from the exit code alone.
findings.location: What section: 'Fold run_ensures() into fsck --repair'; AC: 'a failed unit…never aborts the data-repair'
findings.suggested_fix: Adopt the existing fsck convention: if any ensure unit fails (F > 0), set the fsck exit code to non-zero (e.g., 1), consistent with how issue_count drives the existing exit code. Add an AC: 'rebar fsck --repair with a failing ensure unit exits non-zero and reports F in the summary line.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the 'Fold run_ensures() into fsck --repair' subsection that the ensure phase is skipped (or explicitly permitted) when `_repair_run` returns an abort signal (`_unresolved == -1`); the plan should state the intended behavior to avoid implementer ambiguity.
findings.finding: The plan places the ensure sweep (calling `run_ensures`) in the `if do_repair:` block after `_repair_run()` returns, but `_repair_run` has multiple abort paths that return `(lines, -1)` (reconciler in-flight, git-add failure, commit failure, push failure). The plan does not specify whether the ensure phase should be skipped when `_repair_run` aborts. Running `run_ensures` on a half-repaired or abort-state store (where data-repair was rolled back or partially applied) is semantically questionable: ensures may write `.ensure-applied` markers against a store that did not finish data-repair, making it appear converged when it is not.
findings.location: What section — 'Fold run_ensures() into fsck --repair' subsection; fsck.py:620–674 (_repair_run abort paths)
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'If `_repair_run` returns an abort signal (`_unresolved == -1`), skip the ensure phase and surface only the abort lines; do not call `run_ensures` against a store whose data-repair was not completed.'

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the contradiction between Scope (no_mutate-never-sweep is WS5's domain) and AC bullet 5 (WS3's test file covers no_mutate-never-sweep): either move the no_mutate AC assertion to WS5's scope or explicitly carve out 'basic no_mutate-never-sweep wiring check' as WS3's and 'authoritative behavioral suite' as WS5's.
findings.finding: The Scope section declares 'no_mutate-never-sweep behavioral suite (WS5)' as OUT of scope for WS3, but AC bullet 5 explicitly assigns 'no_mutate-never-sweep' test coverage to WS3's new test file — a direct contradiction between sections that would send the implementer in two directions.
findings.location: Scope section ('Out:') vs. Acceptance Criteria bullet 5
findings.suggested_fix: In AC bullet 5, qualify: 'covers the N/M line, --repair sweep + fsck-level idempotency, and a basic no_mutate-never-sweep wiring assertion (the authoritative behavioral suite lives in WS5).' Remove 'no_mutate-never-sweep' from the Scope out-list or add a parenthetical distinguishing basic wiring from authoritative suite.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State a concrete default timeout (e.g. `timeout=5.0`) for the `run_ensures` call in MCP server startup so the executor does not have to guess.
findings.finding: The MCP-startup lock timeout is specified only as 'a few seconds' and the run_ensures call shows `timeout=…` (a literal ellipsis), leaving the executor to choose the concrete value without a stated default.
findings.location: What > MCP server startup paragraph; also AC4
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `timeout=…` with a specific value in the plan, e.g. `run_ensures(tracker, timeout=5.0, attempts=1)`, and note it in AC4 as the expected lock budget.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state whether `run_ensures()` (WS1) and `applied_ids()` (read path) share the same absent/garbage-marker fallback semantics (`set()`), or add a cross-reference to the WS1 spec section that governs this, so the `_scan` N/M result is provably consistent with the post-`--repair` state.
findings.finding: The decision rule for the `_scan`-path N/M computation references `ensures.applied_ids(tracker)` and `ensures.registry_ids()` as concrete callables, but neither the module nor the function signatures exist in the codebase. More critically, the plan never specifies what `applied_ids` returns when the `.ensure-applied` marker file is absent vs. malformed (beyond the one-sentence 'An absent/garbage marker yields `applied_ids == set()`'), and co-locates this fallback rule only in the read-only `_scan` description without stating whether `run_ensures` in `--repair` also uses the same fallback — creating a protocol gap between the two paths.
findings.location: What section — 'Read-only `ensures: N/M applied` line': "An absent/garbage marker yields `applied_ids == set()` → `ensures: 0/M applied`"

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the log level (e.g., WARNING) and at least one structured field (e.g., `event='ensure_sweep_skipped'`) for MCP startup ensure failures, so operators can search/alert on them.
findings.finding: MCP server startup ensure failures are described as 'log-and-continue' but the plan does not specify the log level, logger name, or structured fields — operators cannot easily find or alert on a skipped/failed startup sweep without knowing where to look.
findings.location: MCP server startup section: 'Wrap the whole thing in a try/except that log-and-continues'
findings.suggested_fix: Log at WARNING with a structured field, e.g.: `logger.warning('ensure sweep skipped at startup', exc_info=True, extra={'event': 'ensure_sweep_startup_failed'})` — this makes the event grep-able and alertable without changing the best-effort semantics.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out path for the fsck --repair behavioral change — e.g., document how to revert to pre-WS3 behavior (feature flag, a revert commit reference, or an expand-contract note stating the sweep is additive-only and marker files can be manually removed to reset state).
findings.finding: The plan acknowledges the behavioral change to existing `fsck --repair` callers ('an acknowledged behavioral change') but provides no rollback path, back-out plan, or opt-out mechanism. T4 requires acknowledgement plus a reversibility mechanism — acknowledgement alone is insufficient.
findings.location: What section — 'Fold run_ensures() into fsck --repair' paragraph
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Rollback' note: 'The ensure sweep is purely additive (writes .ensure-applied markers only). To back out: delete the .ensure-applied marker files to reset ensure state; no ticket data is modified by the sweep. A revert of the WS3 commit restores the prior --repair behavior.' This satisfies T4's reversibility requirement.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the write-order contract for `.ensure-applied` relative to unit side-effects: define whether the marker is written before or after side-effects (and why), require atomic marker writes (write-to-tmp + rename), and assert the schema state of `.ensure-applied` after a partial failure so that a crashed mid-sweep run leaves the store in a resumable (not silently-skipped) state.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify the write order or atomicity semantics for `.ensure-applied` during `run_ensures()`: if the process is killed after the marker records a unit as applied but before (or during) that unit's side-effects are fully committed and pushed, the marker permanently marks the unit as done. A subsequent `fsck --repair` skips it (idempotency gating reads the marker), silently leaving the store un-converged with no indication of the partial failure. The plan's 'log-and-continue on failed outcome' only covers errors caught by `run_ensures` itself, not mid-sweep crashes.
findings.location: What section — 'Fold run_ensures() into fsck --repair'; AC2 (fsck-level idempotency check); AC1 ('run_ensures acquires AND RELEASES the store write lock INTERNALLY')
findings.suggested_fix: Require `run_ensures` to follow apply-then-mark order (side-effects committed before the unit id is appended to `.ensure-applied`) with an atomic JSON rewrite (write to `.ensure-applied.tmp` then `os.replace`). Units whose side-effects fail or are interrupted are NOT added to the marker; the next `--repair` run retries them. Add this invariant explicitly to WS1's `run_ensures` spec and cross-reference it in WS3's AC2.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one concrete proving command per AC (e.g. `pytest tests/interfaces/store/test_fsck_ensures.py -v`, a `rebar fsck --repair` invocation against a fixture showing the report line, and a `--dry-run` invocation asserting no `.ensure-applied` write) so each criterion produces verifiable evidence.
findings.finding: No concrete proving commands are provided for any acceptance criterion — the plan states observable outcomes but leaves 'how to produce evidence' entirely implicit, violating E6(a).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (all five ACs)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a measurement rationale or probe for the MCP startup lock-timeout value (e.g. measure p99 lock-hold time during a repair run on a representative store, or at minimum document the acceptable startup-delay budget that drove the 'few seconds' choice).
findings.finding: The MCP startup lock timeout ('a few seconds') is an asserted heuristic with no measurement plan or derivation — it is stated without data on what contention window is acceptable or how it was chosen.
findings.location: MCP server startup section: 'a SHORT lock budget (a few seconds)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Make the MCP startup lock timeout/attempts configurable (e.g. via an env var, config key, or named constant with a comment) rather than a hardcoded literal.
findings.finding: The MCP startup lock timeout ('a few seconds') is hardcoded in the implementation, not surfaced as a configurable parameter — a value expected to need tuning in different deployment environments should be configurable.
findings.location: MCP server startup section / run_ensures timeout/attempts passthrough description
findings.suggested_fix: Introduce a module-level constant (e.g. `MCP_ENSURE_LOCK_TIMEOUT_S = 3`) or a config key so the value is findable and changeable without hunting for the literal.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Capture the decision to fold run_ensures into fsck --repair (rather than add a new flag) in a CLAUDE.md entry, ADR, or a prominent comment block in fsck.py so the rationale is durable and discoverable.
findings.finding: The acknowledged behavioral change to `rebar fsck --repair` (existing callers now also converge config) is a non-trivial architectural decision that is not captured in any ADR, CLAUDE.md entry, or design-doc update.
findings.location: What section: 'existing `fsck --repair` callers now ALSO converge config — an acknowledged behavioral change'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a short ADR entry or a `# Design: ensures sweep is folded into --repair (not a separate flag) because ...` comment block at the top of the relevant fsck.py section, referencing this workstream.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the retirement path for stale `.ensure-applied` marker entries (e.g., document that pruning is deferred, or specify that `fsck --repair` prunes IDs no longer in registry_ids()) so the lifecycle is complete.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no retirement or pruning path for stale entries in the `.ensure-applied` marker file when ensure units are removed from the registry. Over time, the marker file accumulates IDs for units that no longer exist.
findings.location: What section: 'Read-only ensures: N/M applied line'; phrase 'intersection so a stale marker id can't inflate N'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that the `timeout`/`attempts` passthrough on `run_ensures` is justified by ≥2 active non-default callers, or consider hard-coding the short lock budget at the MCP call-site (e.g., via an inline `write_lock(..., attempts=1)` wrapper) to avoid parameterizing `run_ensures` for a single use case.
findings.finding: The `timeout`/`attempts` passthrough added to `run_ensures` (WS3 adds it as an 'optional passthrough') has exactly 2 call-sites at plan time: `fsck --repair` (which does NOT use the override — it runs with defaults) and MCP startup. Only MCP startup actually passes non-default values. Rule-of-Three: a parametric abstraction with a single active non-default caller is premature. The parameter could instead be hard-coded at the MCP call-site if `run_ensures` accepted a simpler lock-budget argument, or the MCP path could just call `write_lock` directly with `attempts=1` before delegating.
findings.location: What section — 'WS3 adds an optional `timeout`/`attempts` passthrough to `run_ensures` that forwards to `write_lock`; default `None` keeps WS1's behavior unchanged'
findings.suggested_fix: At the MCP startup call-site, have the caller acquire the lock with `attempts=1` and a short timeout before calling `run_ensures` (already lock-free from the caller's perspective), rather than threading timeout parameters through `run_ensures` itself. This removes the `None`-sentinel convention and keeps `run_ensures`'s signature simple.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Audit `tests/integration/test_concurrency_regression.py`, `tests/interfaces/lifecycle/test_compact_negative.py`, and `tests/interfaces/store/test_tracker_config_e2e.py` for stdout assertions on `fsck_cli` output, and enumerate them in the plan's test-impact section.
findings.finding: Three existing direct callers of `fsck_cli` / `_scan` outside `fsck.py`'s own directory are not enumerated as consumers: `tests/integration/test_concurrency_regression.py`, `tests/interfaces/lifecycle/test_compact_negative.py`, and `tests/interfaces/store/test_tracker_config_e2e.py`. All three call `fsck_cli(...)` in-process and capture stdout; the new `ensures: N/M applied` line added to `_scan` changes the captured output and may cause assertion failures.
findings.location: What section — 'Read-only `ensures: N/M applied` line … Because this lives in `_scan`, the library read-only surface … prints the line'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief justification in the plan for why boot-time convergence in mcp_server.main is preferred over existing alternatives (e.g., a post-init advisory or relying solely on `fsck --repair`), or explicitly acknowledge the trade-off of write-lock acquisition on every boot.
findings.finding: The MCP server boot-sweep (calling run_ensures on every server start) is a novel architectural pattern with no prior-art justification. An established alternative — convergence only on explicit CLI invocation (fsck --repair) with no boot-time write side effect — exists in the same codebase and is not discussed. The plan asserts best-effort safety but does not address why boot-time convergence is preferred over a simpler 'run fsck --repair once after init' advisory.
findings.location: What section — 'MCP server startup' paragraph; AC line 4

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
